title: Harvesting honey: uncapping and extraction
subtitle: How to remove supers, uncap the comb, and extract honey by centrifuge.
excerpt: Honey is ready to harvest when the bees have capped at least 75 percent of the cells in a super. This tutorial covers removing supers, uncapping the wax, running the extractor, and getting the honey to jar. It takes two hours for a first-year beekeeper with one or two supers.
sourceNotes: National Bee Unit, Honey Handling Guide (2024). BBKA Good Practice Guide, harvesting section. Food Standards Agency, Honey Hygiene Regulations (England, 2015). Ted Hooper, Guide to Bees and Honey (1976), extraction chapter. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: Honey sealed under a thin white wax capping by the bees. The bees cap a cell when the water content of the honey has dropped to around 20 percent or below. Uncapped or wet-looking honey has too much water and will ferment.

A wide comb of thin metal tines used to scratch or lift the wax cappings from a honey frame before extraction. Faster for large areas; a heated uncapping knife is the alternative for heavy capped surfaces.

A centrifuge that holds frames in a cartwheel pattern, spokes pointing inward. Spinning flings honey from both sides of the frame simultaneously. Faster than a tangential extractor but typically more expensive.

A board with a one-way bee escape fitted, placed between the super and the brood box the evening before harvest. Bees move down through the escape but cannot return. The super is clear of bees by the following morning.

A small optical instrument that measures the water content of honey. A reading below 20 percent means the honey is safe to jar. Above 20 percent carries a fermentation risk. Beekeeper refractometers are calibrated specifically for honey.

content.text: The right moment to harvest is when the frames are at least 75 percent covered in
capped honey
. Tap a frame over a clean surface and listen. Ripe honey stays in the cells; runny uncapped honey drips out. If you have one, check the water content with a
refractometer
before you jar. Above 20 percent water, the honey will ferment in the jar within weeks.
Clearing the super of bees
The cleanest method is to use a
clearer board

. Fit it under the super the evening before harvest. Most bees will have descended by the following morning. Remove the super, seal it with a cloth or board, and carry it indoors. Keep it covered until you extract. Even a few minutes of an open honey super outdoors in summer will start a robbing frenzy from other colonies.

If you do not have a clearer board, brush the bees from each frame individually using the bee brush before removing it. Work quickly. Keep the smoker lit. The bees that stayed behind in the box become defensive quickly once the super is lifted.
Uncapping the frames
Work over a large food-grade tray or an uncapping tank. Hold the frame vertically by the top bar. Draw the
uncapping fork

upwards from the bottom of the frame, lifting the cappings off without digging into the comb. Lean the frame against the side of the tray and uncap the second side. Cappings fall into the tray and can be melted down for wax later.
A heated uncapping knife gives a cleaner cut on frames with an even surface. Move it slowly. The blade just needs to be warm enough to slice the wax, not melt it deeply into the comb.
Loading and running the extractor
In a
radial extractor

, load frames in pairs on opposite sides to keep the drum balanced. Spin slowly for the first minute to avoid breaking the comb, then increase speed. One to two minutes at moderate speed is enough for a well-capped frame. Honey collects at the bottom of the drum and drains through a tap into a settling tank or bucket below.

attrs.title: Tangential vs radial extractors
attrs.body: A tangential extractor holds frames facing outward and only extracts one side at a time. You spin, turn all frames, then spin again. It is cheaper and adequate for a small apiary. A radial extractor does both sides at once and is better for larger harvests. Both work. Speed matters less than balance: an unbalanced drum damages frames and stresses the equipment.

content.text: Filtering and settling

Pour the raw honey through a double-layer filter or a fine mesh strainer into a clean food-grade settling tank. Leave it for 24 to 48 hours. Wax particles and air bubbles rise to the surface; skim them off before jarring. Do not heat honey above 40°C at any point. Heat destroys the enzymes that give fresh honey its flavour and antimicrobial properties.
Returning the wet supers

After extraction the frames are sticky and wet with residual honey. Place them back on the hive in the evening. The bees will clean them overnight. Remove the super the next day and store the dry frames in a sealed container or bag to prevent wax moth damage. Do not store wet frames without giving the bees a chance to clean them first.

entries.cue: Honey will not drip from frames even after uncapping
entries.cause: Honey is cold; room temperature is below 20°C
entries.fix: Warm the extraction room to 25 to 30°C before you start. Set frames near a gentle heat source for an hour. Never microwave or heat honey over a flame.

entries.cue: Frames keep breaking in the extractor
entries.cause: Spinning too fast on cold or newly drawn comb
entries.fix: Start the extractor very slowly, under half speed, for the first 30 seconds. Increase gradually. Old drawn comb handles speed better than new comb built that same season.

entries.cue: Honey crystallises in the settling tank before jarring
entries.cause: High glucose content in the honey, or cool room temperature; common in oilseed rape honey
entries.fix: Warm the tank gently in a water bath at 35°C to re-liquify. Jar oilseed rape honey within 24 hours of extraction before it sets solid in the tank.
